result: I keep coming back to the idea that every object is a clock. The coffee mug on my desk ticks off ceramic years, its glaze crazing like river deltas under a magnifying glass. My grandmother’s thimbles in the sewing box measure generations in oxidized fingerprints. Even the dust is a metronome, falling at six inches per century onto the abandoned piano we never learned to play. We think time is something we travel through, but it’s more like a substance we leave behind, stratum after stratum of skin cells and forgotten passwords. Last night I watched a moth beat against the porch light, each collision a soft percussion, a cymbal tap announcing the interval between now and the moment its wings wear transparent. I wanted to tell it that photons are older than the sun, that the light it seeks already ghosted through dinosaur pupils, but the moth has its own calendar, inked in pheromones and lunar angles. Meanwhile my phone updates itself into another adolescence, icons rearranging like shy students switching desks. I press delete on years of screenshots and feel the hush that follows a tree falling in a forest where no one’s browsing. Somewhere between the mug and the moth I realize memory isn’t storage but weather—warm front meets cold, condensation of almost-true. Tomorrow the forecast says increasing probability of regret with scattered awe. I’ll open the curtains, let the old light in, and try to read the future by the layers still clinging to the glass.
